chunks.text: Every day, we are exposed to an enormous amount of information.
Newspapers, television, article on the internet, and posts on social media.
But how much of it is truly accurate?
An ad exists not to convey fact, but to encourage purchase.
Political propaganda manipulates information in order to spread a particular viewpoint.
Now anyone can publish information on social media.
As a result, unverified rumors and misleading information spread instantly.
The more sensational the headline, the more attention it draws.
First, verify the source of the information.
Who published it, and what evidence does it rest upon?
If statistics are quoted, have the eye to analyze how that data was gathered.
It is also important to compare several sources for any single event.
If we judge from only one perspective, we arrive at a biased conclusion.
Critical thinking is the only power that protects us in this sea of information.
Media literacy has become an essential skill for everyone living in our age.
It is not about doubting information, but about facing information in the right way.
